data.title: Copper hits record high above $14,000 as speculators pile in
data.description:
By Eric Onstad

Copper prices hit a record high of more than $14,000 a metric ton on Thursday, as speculators extended their buying spree, encouraged by expectations of strong demand and supported by a weak dollar and geopolitical concerns.

They ignored warnings by some analysts that high prices would chill physical demand by industrial consumers and was not being supported by current supply/demand fundamentals.

Benchmark three-month copper on the London Metal Exchange jumped 7.9% to an all-time high of $14,125 a metric ton during Asian trading.

It pared gains to $13,935 a ton by 1050 GMT, a rise of 6.5%.

"Copper posted its biggest one-day gain in years... driven by intense speculative trading by bulls in China," Neil Welsh at Britannia Global Markets said in a note.

"Investors are piling into base metals on expectations for stronger U.S. growth and more global spending on data centres, robotics and power infrastructure."

Copper, used in power and construction, is a key metal needed for the energy transition, but global exchange-monitored inventories are at high levels, especially in the U.S.

The most-active copper contract on the Shanghai Futures Exchange closed daytime trading 6.7% higher at 109,110 yuan ($15,708.77) a ton, after setting a record of 110,970 yuan.

The gains came despite weak spot physical demand in the biggest consumer market China. The Yangshan copper premium (SMM-CUYP-CN), a gauge of Chinese demand for imported copper, declined to $20 a ton on Wednesday, the lowest since July 2024 and down from $55 in December.

Copper is also rising due to a spillover of interest for hard assets, which have sent gold and silver to records, partly due to geopolitical tensions, traders said.

Also supporting metals was a weaker dollar index , which was close to multi-year lows, making commodities priced in the U.S. currency cheaper for buyers using other currencies.

LME aluminium gained 1.5% to $3,307 a ton, zinc surged 3.8% to $3,493, lead added 1.3% to $2,044, nickel jumped 4.2% to $19,045 and tin rose 0.7% to $56,350.

($1 = 6.9458 Chinese yuan renminbi)

data.title: US, African Union Launch Strategic Investment Working Group
data.description:
JERUSALEM (dpa-AFX) - The United States and African Union have agreed to launch a Strategic Infrastructure and Investment Working Group to promote and advance U.S.-Africa economic partnerships that create jobs, prosperity, and economic security both in the U.S. and across Africa.

The agreement to establish a U.S.-AUC Strategic Infrastructure and Investment Working Group was signed in a meeting between U.S. Deputy Secretary of State Christopher Landau and AUC Chairperson Mahmoud Ali Youssouf at the African Union Commission in Addis Ababa to reaffirm the importance of the U.S.-Africa relationship through the African Union.

The SIWG will serve as a platform for senior officials and technical experts across the U.S. government and AUC to identify and advance opportunities for U.S. private sector investment and engagement in AU-backed infrastructure projects and related initiatives that advance the shared strategic priorities of the United States and the AU, its member states, and Africa's Regional Economic Communities - enabling trade and logistics infrastructure, as well as continent-wide digital transformation.

These investments will leverage AU convening authority and expertise alongside U.S. capital and innovative financing tools to develop critical minerals and commodities supply chains, transportation corridors, energy networks, and regulatory harmonization, as well as to increase two-way trade, secure digital infrastructure, and improve health security that will make Americans and Africans safer and more prosperous.

As the United States and Africa seek durable, profitable investments to drive economic goals in place of foreign assistance, the SIWG will provide a foundation for strategic economic cooperation that will grow and shape the relationship for years to come, says a joint statement issued by the United States and African Union.

data.title: SAP valuation flips from overegged to undercooked
data.description:
By Jennifer Johnson

The $250 bln software giant last year sold off with software peers amid an AI panic. Back then SAP’s valuation looked toppy given its core appeal – a backlog of clients to go on the cloud – is finite. Yet even though that cohort is clearing more slowly, it’s still there.

Full view will be published shortly.

Follow Jennifer Johnson on Bluesky and LinkedIn.

CONTEXT NEWS

SAP said growth in its current cloud backlog (CCB) will decelerate this year after a 25% expansion in 2025. Management had previously guided for 26% full-year CCB growth at its third-quarter results.

SAP shares were down 14% to 169 euros as of 1015 GMT on January 29.

The software group reported total revenue of 9.7 billion euros ($11.6 billion) for the final quarter of last year, in line with a company-compiled consensus. It also announced a two-year buyback programme worth up to 10 billion euros.

data.title: Carlyle Edges Ahead in Race for Lukoil's International Assets — WSJ
data.description:
By Joe Wallace and Ben Dummett

Carlyle struck a provisional deal to buy sanctioned Russian energy giant Lukoil's international assets, potentially handing the private-equity firm a sprawling portfolio of oil fields, refineries and gas stations.

Lukoil announced the agreement Thursday but didn't disclose a price and said it was still negotiating with other potential buyers. The deal would also require signoff from the U.S. Treasury Department, which has acted as gatekeeper on the sale since blacklisting the Russian energy producer in the fall.

The provisional deal is the latest twist in a chaotic sales process for a global energy empire that spans American gas stations, refiners in Europe and oil fields in the Middle East and Central Asia.

The Trump administration spiked an earlier deal for Gunvor to buy the Lukoil assets, describing the Swiss trading firm as a puppet for the Kremlin — a characterization Gunvor rejected as "fundamentally misinformed and false."

Carlyle, based a few blocks from the White House, could face a smoother ride in Washington. But the buyout firm does face competition to secure a deal. Others to have sought U.S. permission to bid for the assets include private-equity mogul Todd Boehly. The Treasury has twice extended the deadline for potential bidders.

The Austrian subsidiary that houses Lukoil's international operations has valued the assets in filings at $22 billion. However, U.S. sanctions turned Russia's second-biggest oil company into a pariah on international markets, making the business hard to assess. The price Carlyle could pay for the assets has yet to be determined, pending its due diligence, according to a person familiar with the agreement.

To stop Russia from benefiting from a sale, the U.S. has said the eventual buyer can't release any funds to Lukoil until sanctions are lifted. Carlyle said that its proposed transaction was structured to be fully compliant with the U.S. government's policies.

Thursday's agreement with Carlyle doesn't cover Lukoil's assets in Kazakhstan. Those include 5% of the huge Tengiz oil field, in which Chevron and Exxon Mobil hold stakes. The Kazakh government said this week that it had asked the U.S. for permission to buy out Lukoil's interest in Tengiz and other joint projects in the country.

Carlyle has retained deep exposure to the oil-and-gas industry even as Blackstone and other buyout firms have backed away. Over the years, it has invested in drillers and refiners in Europe, Africa and Latin America.

Carlyle oversees total assets of $474 billion, of which $20 billion is earmarked for energy, renewables and infrastructure. Current investments include VaroPreem, a European-based refinery operator, Moeve, an oil-and-gas company based in Spain, and SierraCol Energy, an oil producer in Colombia.

Even so, an agreement with Lukoil would be transformational for the private-equity group, handing it one of the biggest energy portfolios in the world. Carlyle itself has a market capitalization of $22 billion.

Carlyle's strategy for Lukoil's assets couldn't be learned. One approach would be to team up with oil traders who could help operate the assets as well as buy and sell petroleum from them. Carlyle has worked closely with London-based commodities merchant Vitol on their European refinery joint-venture, Varo.

The deal announcement was another unusual development in an M&A process with few parallels. Typically, companies wouldn't announce a sale until they were in exclusive talks. Thursday's agreement gives both Lukoil and Carlyle the wiggle room to back out.

Carlyle will need Washington's blessing to land the deal. It has ties to both political parties. Co-founder David Rubenstein once advised Jimmy Carter while Glenn Youngkin, until recently the governor of Virginia, entered Republican politics after running Carlyle as co-chief executive.

Write to Joe Wallace at joe.wallace@wsj.com and Ben Dummett at ben.dummett@wsj.com

data.title: Chemicals maker Dow to eliminate 4,500 roles, targets $2 billion core profit boost
data.description:
Dow said on Thursday it would cut about 4,500 jobs as part of a sweeping restructuring aimed at boosting profitability by at least $2 billion, as the chemicals maker confronts weak demand and industrywide structural pressures.

Shares of the company rose 3.3% in premarket trading.

The company expects to incur about $1.1 billion to $1.5 billion in one-time costs tied to the restructuring.

The Michigan-based company reported an adjusted loss of 34 cents per share for the quarter ended December 31, compared with analysts' average estimate of a loss of 46 cents, according to data compiled by LSEG.

data.title: The 10-Point: The Wall Street Journal's Guide to the Day's Top News
data.description:
By Emma Tucker

After the shooting of Alex Pretti in Minneapolis, President Trump's advisers followed their boss's playbook for moments of crisis: Cede no ground, never admit mistakes and attack. But this time the strategy backfired. Now, the president and his aides are rushing to limit the political damage, pointing fingers and shifting their strategy as they go. The killing has shaken the nation-and provoked a clash in the White House over the future of Trump's immigration policy. And for Gregory Bovino, the operation that was supposed to be his biggest yet led to his dramatic fall.

Today's Headlines

Minneapolis residents and local law enforcement are forging common ground as Minnesota faces off with federal authorities.

The arrest of Ryan Wedding — a former Olympian and alleged drug lord — involved a secret FBI operation and rocked U.S. relations with Mexico.

Big companies from Amazon.com to UPS are slashing jobs, looking to shrink their head counts after years of breakneck growth.

Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell has declined to say whether he will remain on the central bank's board after his chairmanship ends. His silence is strategic.

Senate Democrats publicly laid out their demands for an overhaul of immigration enforcement days ahead of a deadline to pass a funding package that would avert a partial government shutdown.

Live From The Markets

Billionaire media and tech investor Barry Diller approached Warner Bros. Discovery last year expressing interest in buying CNN.

Quarterly reports from Meta Platforms and Microsoft showed the two AI spending champs on different trajectories, writes Dan Gallagher.

Read It Here First

Trump's border chief was a rising star. Then he arrived in Minneapolis.

For six months, Gregory Bovino was the face of rolling immigration raids that netted thousands of arrests. His Minnesota campaign, "Operation Metro Surge," was supposed to be his most ambitious mission yet. Instead, it descended into scenes of tear gas, pepper spray and civil unrest. Less than 48 hours after referring to border agents who killed Pretti as "victims," Bovino was pulled from the field. With his removal, some are anticipating an end to the maximalist enforcement strategy he championed, report Joshua Chaffin and Michelle Hackman.

A battered Iran is still able to mount a deadly response to U.S. strikes.

Israel pounded Iran's military in June's war, but the regime emerged from the bruising conflict with much of its arsenal of thousands of ballistic missiles intact, which gives it the ability to inflict real damage on the U.S. and its allies. Tehran's arsenal gives it the potential to hit targets around the Middle East, meaning any attack by the U.S. could quickly escalate despite Trump's preference for decisive use of force that doesn't turn into a regional conflict, report Laurence Norman and Dov Lieber.

Expert Take

Q: Why is the dollar sliding?

The currency's drop has renewed fears that America's pre-eminent position in global markets is waning, write David Uberti and Chelsey Dulaney.

A: The dollar's strength over the past decade was propelled in large part by "American exceptionalism," or the outperformance of the U.S. economy and markets compared with the rest of the world. Political stability and strong rule of law gave overseas investors extra reason to snap up U.S. assets.

Both sources of support are weakening somewhat.

While economists have dialed up U.S. growth projections, stimulative economic policies in Japan, Germany and elsewhere have helped overseas stock markets in recent months outperform their counterparts stateside. At the same time, even after the Federal Reserve held interest rates steady yesterday, many on Wall Street expect the central bank to resume easing policy in 2026, as Trump has advocated.

More cuts could narrow the premium that U.S. Treasury yields offer relative to government debt in other countries, drawing investors elsewhere to help shield themselves from the risk of owning U.S. assets like stocks.

That is why much of Wall Street is betting on more dollar weakness to come, potentially ending a yearslong run in which the currency enticed investors the world over to park more money in America.

A weaker dollar has always been part of Trump's plan, says Greg Ip.

See The Story

A Storybook cottage gets a fairy tale ending.

When Flor Mora closed on her new home for $4 million in November 2025, she knew she was acquiring a piece of California history. What she didn't realize was that she was also buying the future WSJ House of the Year. The M.J. Murphy-designed home, built in 1925 and located in Carmel Highlands, is an example of early 20th-century Storybook architecture. The style is synonymous with the neighboring city of Carmel-by-the-Sea, which is known for its fairy tale aesthetic.

Happening Today

The European Union is expected to designate Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps as a terror organization, a step that the U.S. has long called for.

Economic data: U.S. trade deficit; weekly jobless claims

Earnings: Apple, Blackstone, Caterpillar, Comcast, Lockheed Martin, Mastercard, Visa

Number Of The Day:

79

The life expectancy at birth for the average American in 2024, a record high, according to a new report from the National Center for Health Statistics. The increase signals a rebound from declines in life expectancy during the coronavirus pandemic and progress in combating the opioid crisis.

And Finally...

Everybody is suddenly obsessed with 2016.

A severe bout of nostalgia is spreading in America, but it's not for the postwar prosperity of the 1950s or the big hair and bright colors of the '80s. Young adults are yearning for the simple days of 2016. For weeks, social-media feeds have been littered with decade-old images of Snapchat filters and skinny jeans. The internet obsession is spilling into real life: The top musical artists of that year are enjoying a new surge in popularity and clubs are hosting 2016-themed parties.

data.title: Irish GDP Contracted 0.6% in Q4
data.description:
Ireland’s gross domestic product fell by 0.6% quarter-on-quarter in the three months to December 2025, following a 0.3% decline in the previous quarter, according to preliminary estimates.

This marked the second consecutive period of economic contraction, largely driven by a downturn in the multinational-dominated industrial sector.

On an annual basis, the economy expanded by 3.7%, a significant slowdown from the 10.8% growth recorded in the previous quarter, and the weakest annual increase since the economic contraction in the second quarter of 2024.

data.title: French Unemployment Falls for 2nd Month
data.description:
The number of people registered as unemployed in France dropped by 11.9 thousand in December of 2025 to 3.117 million, the lowest in three months.

Unemployment fell by 12.4 thousand from the previous month to 505 thousand for the population with less than 25 years of age, while it inched lower by 700 individuals to 808 thousand for the population above 50 years.

In turn, unemployment grew by 1.2 thousand to 1.814 million for the core aged population.

data.title: Ethio Telecom half-year revenue rises 37% to $548 million
data.description:
Ethiopia's state-owned Ethio Telecom posted a 37% rise in half-year revenue to 85.02 billion birr ($548.30 million) in the six months to December 2025, after its customer base grew and usage increased, Chief Executive Frehiwot Tamiru said on Thursday.

The telecoms operator reported a net profit of 42.36 billion birr for the period, achieving a gross profit margin of 49.8%.

Subscriber numbers grew 8.2% year-on-year to 87.1 million, Tamiru told a press conference.

Ethio Telecom, which the government plans to privatise as part of a broader economic liberalisation of the tightly-controlled Horn of Africa economy, held an initial public offering last year but sold only 10.7% of the shares offered.

The company expects full-year revenue to jump 45.5% in the year to June 2026.

($1 = 155.0611 birr)

data.title: Deezer licenses AI music detection tool to French royalty agency Sacem, plans wider rollout
data.description:
By Leo Marchandon and Jaspreet Singh

Music streaming platform Deezer has licensed its artificial intelligence-detection technology to France's royalty agency Sacem in a landmark commercial deal to combat music fraud, as the company pursues wider industry adoption for the tool.

The deal, announced on Thursday, comes as expanding AI capabilities blur the line between human- and machine-made songs, enabling a new type of streaming fraud where bad actors upload thousands of AI-generated numbers designed to trigger algorithmic recommendations and siphon royalties away from artists and songwriters.

France-based Deezer said it successfully identified and removed up to 85% of fraudulent AI-generated music streams from its royalty pool in 2025, flagging over 13.4 million AI tracks.

The platform now receives around 60,000 fully AI-created tracks every day, roughly 39% of total daily uploads, up from 10% in January last year.

Deezer's royalty pool comprises 70% of subscriber revenue, according to CEO Alexis Lanternier.

The detection tool analyzes audio signals for patterns created by AI music generators such as Suno and Udio, identifying subtle anomalies inaudible to human ears. The company has trained the system on 94 million songs and filed two patents for the technology in 2024, it said.

However, Swedish royalty society Stim told Reuters that detection tools alone cannot be an answer to issues surrounding musical composition and copyright. "We believe that copyright and technology can go hand in hand," Stim said.

Stim, which in 2025 launched its license that allows AI companies to legally use copyrighted songs for training models, believes mandatory licensing and full transparency for training data would prevent fraud at its source.

Deezer is in discussions with other European collective societies regarding licensing and plans to engage with organizations in Los Angeles during Grammy Week, Lanternier said.

Sacem did not reply to a Reuters request for comment.
